description:
You are a support-desk automation engineer.  
Create a very small “diagnostics kit” that can be run with one command and that always produces the **same, fully-predictable artefacts** so that an automated test suite can validate the result.

Directory layout that must exist when you are finished:

/home/user/diag/  
├─ Makefile  
└─ output/  
   ├─ sysinfo.log  
   ├─ diskusage.log  
   ├─ processes.log  
   └─ diag_report.tar.gz  

Required Makefile targets (case-sensitive):

1. sysinfo        – generates /home/user/diag/output/sysinfo.log  
2. diskusage      – generates /home/user/diag/output/diskusage.log  
3. processes      – generates /home/user/diag/output/processes.log  
4. archive        – produces /home/user/diag/output/diag_report.tar.gz  
5. clean          – removes every file inside /home/user/diag/output/ (but keeps the directory)  
6. all (default)  – sequentially runs sysinfo, diskusage, processes, then archive  

For ease of grading, **do not make the archive name dynamic**; it must always be exactly
diag_report.tar.gz and must contain only the three *.log files, no sub-directories.

Exact, line-for-line log file specification
-------------------------------------------

Each log file must contain **exactly three lines** (newline at the end of every line).

1. /home/user/diag/output/sysinfo.log  
   Line 1: === SYSINFO ===  
   Line 2: The output of the command “uname -srm” (no extra words).  
   Line 3: The UTC date/time in ISO-8601 “YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS UTC” format produced with  
           date -u +"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S UTC"

2. /home/user/diag/output/diskusage.log  
   Line 1: === DISKUSAGE ===  
   Line 2: The output of “df -h / | tail -1 | awk '{print $5}'” (this is the root-fs usage %).  
   Line 3: Same ISO-8601 UTC timestamp format as above.

3. /home/user/diag/output/processes.log  
   Line 1: === TOPMEM PROCESSES ===  
   Line 2: The numeric PID of the process using the most resident memory, obtained with  
           ps -eo pid,pmem --sort -pmem | head -n 2 | tail -n 1 | awk '{print $1}'  
   Line 3: Same ISO-8601 UTC timestamp format as above.

Makefile implementation rules
-----------------------------

• Every target must create its own log file **only**; do not combine steps.  
• Use the built-in shell commands (no external scripts).  
• Ensure the output directory exists before writing any file.  
• “make clean” must never delete the output directory itself.  
• The “all” target must depend in order: sysinfo diskusage processes archive.  

What the automated grader will check
------------------------------------

1. Existence of /home/user/diag/Makefile.  
2. Running “make -C /home/user/diag clean all” recreates exactly the four files listed above.  
3. Each *.log file has exactly **three newline-terminated lines** with the precise fixed headers shown and a valid timestamp format.  
4. /home/user/diag/output/diag_report.tar.gz is a valid gzip-compressed tar containing only the three log files with **those exact filenames** and byte-for-byte identical contents to the corresponding files on disk.  
5. Re-running “make clean all” repeatedly must always succeed and regenerate identical artefacts.

Hints you may find useful
-------------------------

• The Makefile’s default goal can be set with .DEFAULT_GOAL := all  
• Use the “tar -czf” recipe to build the archive.  
• Wrap variable shell expansions in $(shell …) only when unavoidable; ordinary recipes are fine.  
• Pay attention to tab indentation in Makefile recipes.

When you are done, the grader will run the following high-level sequence:

    cd /home/user/diag
    make clean all
    tar -tzf output/diag_report.tar.gz
    # …and then inspect the individual log files…

No other output or artefacts are expected.
truth:
Expected artefacts AFTER “make clean all” has run successfully:

1. /home/user/diag/Makefile  
   • Contains at least the following targets with colons: sysinfo, diskusage, processes, archive, clean, all  
   • .DEFAULT_GOAL must be either explicit “all” or omitted (GNU make defaults to first target, which must be all).

2. /home/user/diag/output/sysinfo.log  
   • Exactly three lines, terminated by newline.  
   • Line 1: “=== SYSINFO ===”  
   • Line 2: output of uname -srm for the container (e.g. “Linux 5.15.0-1051-azure x86_64”)  
   • Line 3: timestamp matching regex ^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2} [0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2} UTC$

3. /home/user/diag/output/diskusage.log  
   • Exactly three lines.  
   • Line 1: “=== DISKUSAGE ===”  
   • Line 2: something like “5%” (whatever df returns).  
   • Line 3: same timestamp regex.

4. /home/user/diag/output/processes.log  
   • Exactly three lines.  
   • Line 1: “=== TOPMEM PROCESSES ===”  
   • Line 2: a positive integer PID.  
   • Line 3: same timestamp regex.

5. /home/user/diag/output/diag_report.tar.gz  
   • A gzip-compressed tar archive.  
   • tar tzf lists exactly:  
     sysinfo.log  
     diskusage.log  
     processes.log  
   • Each member’s content is byte-for-byte identical to the corresponding file already present on disk.

The grader will tolerate any valid PID, disk-usage percentage, uname string and timestamp values, but WILL insist that the first line of every log file and the archive member list match exactly what is described above.

No other files may live inside /home/user/diag/output/ once “make clean” is executed.

Permissions: All files can be created with default umask (0664 for files, 0775 for dirs). The agent has write access to /home/user/diag and everything under it.
